skill.name: sast-configuration
skill.description: Configure Static Application Security Testing tools for vulnerability detection in application code and CI/CD pipelines.
skill.summary: Set up Semgrep, SonarQube, and CodeQL scanning workflows for application security programs.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command, network, filesystem, credential, and heuristic issues in SKILL.md. Review found these are documentation examples, security-tool terminology, and relative links rather than executable malicious behavior. The skill is safe to publish with low risk because it guides users to run external security tools.

low_findings.title: Documented External Tool Commands
low_findings.description: The command patterns are fenced documentation examples for installing and running Semgrep, SonarQube, CodeQL, and scan workflows. They are not hidden execution logic, but users should review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged commands are shown in Markdown code fences for a SAST configuration guide. No separate script file or automatic execution path is present in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is a Tool Repository Reference
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to the Semgrep pre-commit repository in an example configuration. It does not send data to an external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears inside a pre-commit configuration example and is a public tool source. There is no evidence of credential transfer or network exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Relative Links Misidentified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The parent directory sequences are Markdown links to related skills. They are not file reads, writes, or runtime path construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged strings appear only as documentation links. No code consumes these paths or performs filesystem access.

low_findings.title: Security Keywords Misclassified as Sensitive or Weak Crypto
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM and weak cryptography alerts are keyword collisions in ordinary SAST and SAML documentation text. No credential database access or cryptographic implementation appears.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines describe SAST tooling and enterprise SAML integration. I did not find evidence of SAM database access or weak crypto use.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance and Heuristic Combination Dismissed
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance alert is a Semgrep compliance scan example, and the combined heuristic is caused by benign documentation mentions. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, data exfiltration, or hidden execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The combination exists only because a security configuration guide naturally mentions commands, a repository URL, credentials, and paths. The file contains no override instructions or suspicious control flow.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell and CI examples that users may copy into local environments. This is expected for SAST setup but should be treated as user-reviewed execution guidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples and not automatic actions. They still represent a real operational pattern because users may run them.

content.value_statement: Security teams need repeatable SAST configuration across tools and repositories. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through setup, tuning, and CI/CD integration.
actual_capabilities: Selects SAST tools based on language support and integration needs.
Provides setup guidance for Semgrep, SonarQube, and CodeQL.
Explains CI/CD integration patterns for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins.
Guides custom security rule development and rule tuning.
Recommends quality gates, baselines, and false positive management practices.
Compares SAST tools by use case, language support, cost, and integration fit.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a bundled scanner implementation.
Referenced assets and scripts are described but not present in this scanned package.
Tool versions, pricing, and platform features may change after publication.
Commands require user review before execution in production environments.

use_cases.title: Launch SAST for a New Service
use_cases.description: Create a practical rollout plan with baseline scanning, tool selection, and first quality gates.

use_cases.title: Add Security Scans to CI
use_cases.description: Convert manual scanning requirements into pipeline steps for pull requests and release builds.

use_cases.title: Tune Security Rules for Developers
use_cases.description: Reduce noisy findings and document suppressions while keeping critical issues blocking.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a SAST Tool
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a SAST tool for a repository using [languages]. Compare Semgrep, SonarQube, and CodeQL for my CI environment.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Rollout Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a phased SAST rollout plan for [team size] developers. Include baseline scanning, severity gates, ownership, and false positive handling.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Pipeline Enforcement
prompt_templates.prompt: Design SAST enforcement for [CI platform]. Start with nonblocking scans, then define when findings should block merges.

prompt_templates.title: Develop Custom Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Help design custom SAST rules for [framework or risk pattern]. Include rule intent, test cases, suppression guidance, and rollout steps.

output_examples.input: Set up SAST for a Python API using GitHub Actions.
output: Recommended Semgrep for fast pull request feedback and CodeQL for deeper GitHub-native analysis.
Defined a baseline scan before enforcing failures.
Suggested blocking only critical findings during the first rollout phase.

output_examples.input: Reduce false positives in our SAST program.
output: Grouped noisy rules by source, severity, and affected teams.
Recommended documented suppressions with owners and review dates.
Suggested excluding generated code and test fixtures from blocking gates.

output_examples.input: Compare Semgrep, SonarQube, and CodeQL for enterprise use.
output: Compared each tool by rule customization, language coverage, CI integration, and reporting needs.
Recommended a layered approach when compliance reporting and deep analysis are both required.

best_practices: Run a baseline scan before turning on merge blocking.
Start enforcement with critical and high severity findings only.
Review suppressions regularly and require clear owner documentation.
anti_patterns: Do not block all findings on the first rollout.
Do not scan generated code unless there is a clear reason.
Do not accept suppressions without justification and review dates.

faq.question: Which SAST tools does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers Semgrep, SonarQube, and CodeQL configuration patterns.

faq.question: Can it write custom Semgrep rules?
faq.answer: It can guide rule design, test cases, and tuning decisions.

faq.question: Does it run scans automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides setup guidance and examples that users must review and run.

faq.question: Can it help with CI/CD integration?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes patterns for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, and pre-commit hooks.

faq.question: Is this useful for compliance programs?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports baseline scans, quality gates, reporting workflows, and policy enforcement planning.

faq.question: What should users verify before using commands?
faq.answer: Users should verify tool versions, permissions, tokens, network access, and repository-specific exclusions.
